steps.title: Meet the Parabola
steps.info: The shape of a parabola is defined by two components: its focus and its directrix.
steps.introText: The first conic section we will explore is the parabola. A parabola is the set of all points that are the same distance from the focus (a fixed point) and the directrix (a fixed line).
steps.prompt: Drag around the focus and directrix. Watch the highlighted point, and note that the two dashed distances always remain equal.

steps.title: The Equation of a Parabola
steps.goal: Connect the geometry to the standard equation.
steps.info: When the vertex of a parabola is at the origin, its equation can be expressed in the form x² = 4py. The parameter p represents the distance from the vertex to the focus, while 4p is the coefficient that actually appears in front of y.
steps.introText: Drag the focus and directrix separately. When the vertex is at the origin, an upward-opening parabola has equation x² = 4py; downward-opening uses x² = −4py.
steps.prompt: Try a few positions, then continue once you can comfortably see how p and the equation are linked.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Build a parabola whose equation is exactly x² = 4y.
steps.prompt: Create a parabola with equation x² = 4y, then press Check.

feedback.correct: Perfect! With p = 1, the equation is x² = 4(1)y = 4y.
feedback.incorrect: The equation isn't x² = 4y yet. Remember: p is the distance from the vertex to the focus.
feedback.hint: p = 1, so the focus should be distance 1 away from the vertex.

steps.title: Discover the Vertex
steps.goal: Recognize where the vertex is in relation to the focus and directrix.

choices.label: Halfway between the focus and directrix

steps.feedback: Exactly. The vertex is the midpoint between the focus and the directrix.

steps.title: Wider Parabola
steps.goal: Place the focus at a specific point with the vertex still at the origin.
steps.info: When p = 5, the focus sits five units from the vertex and the parabola opens wider.
steps.prompt: Create a parabola with vertex at (0, 0) and focus at (0, 5), then press Check.

feedback.correct: Great! p = 5, so the equation is x² = 20y.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite — place the focus at (0, 5) with the vertex at the origin.
feedback.hint: Keep the vertex at (0, 0) and move the focus to (0, 5). The directrix should be at y = −5.

steps.title: Read the Equation
steps.goal: Predict how the equation changes when p changes.
steps.prompt: A parabola has vertex at the origin and p = 3. Which equation is correct?
steps.feedback: Right — 4p = 4(3) = 12, so the equation is x² = 12y.

steps.title: Vertex Form
steps.goal: Shift the parabola by placing the vertex at a target location.
steps.info: A more generalized equation for a parabola is (x − h)² = 4p(y − k). The parameter p still means the same thing, but we add two new parameters: h and k. (h, k) is the vertex of the parabola.
steps.prompt: Move the vertex to (2, 1), then press Check.

feedback.correct: Nice! The vertex is at (2, 1) — note how the equation reflects this, where h represents horizontal shift, k represents vertical shift.
feedback.incorrect: The vertex isn't at (2, 1) yet — drag the dashed vertex handle.
feedback.hint: Grab the dashed circle at the vertex and drag it to x = 2, y = 1 on the grid.

steps.title: Narrow Parabola
steps.goal: Build a steep, narrow upward-opening parabola.
steps.info: A narrow parabola has a small p — the focus sits close to the vertex.
steps.prompt: Create a narrow upward-opening parabola with vertex at (0, 0) and p ≤ 1.25, then press Check.

feedback.correct: Well done! A small p gives a narrow, steep parabola.
feedback.incorrect: Try moving the focus closer to the vertex to make the curve narrower.
feedback.hint: Keep the vertex at (0, 0) and place the focus at (0, 1) or closer.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding

choices.label: A parabola is determined only by its focus.
A parabola is determined only by its directrix.
A parabola is determined by both the focus and directrix.
A parabola is determined by its vertex alone.

steps.feedback: The focus and directrix together define every point on the parabola.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Construct four parabolas with specific width and position — no hints.
steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You now understand the basics of parabolas.
